cataloging_subject.label: Laȝamon’s Brut (Otho version)
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged unit is the abridged and reworked Otho version of Laȝamon’s Middle English verse history, represented by British Library Cotton MS Otho C XIII and by the 14,560-row Brook-Leslie/OTA/U-M Otho stream. It is not the abstract work embracing both versions, the longer Caligula version, either physical codex, the 1847 or 1963-1978 editions, the electronic deposits, or the General Corpus XML artifact. Fire damage and editorial damage markers make encoded-row count different from recoverable verse extent; the XML's 146 supplied 100-line groups and 412 printed-page milestones are navigation rather than authorial divisions.

catalog_summary.title: Laȝamon’s Brut (Otho version)
catalog_summary.author: Laȝamon; anonymous Otho reviser
catalog_summary.editor: G. L. Brook and R. F. Leslie
catalog_summary.date: Otho revision before or during production of the surviving witness (1275–1299); exact revision date unknown
catalog_summary.region: England for the surviving Otho witness; exact place of revision unresolved

notes: The parenthetical version label is supplied for discovery and prevents the materially different Caligula version from being collapsed into this record.

titles.value: Layamon's Brut (British Museum Ms. Cotton Otho C.XIII)
notes: The historical institution name and shelfmark punctuation are retained from the U-M discovery title; the current holding institution is the British Library.

Goering identifies Brut as the modern conventional title; the surviving manuscripts also circulate historical Latin title forms, but this review does not assign one to a specific Otho rubric without direct image evidence.

agents.name: Laȝamon
notes: Author of the underlying Brut; the Otho stream is an abridgement and reworking whose exact revising agency is not named.

agents.name: Anonymous Otho reviser
notes: A descriptive agent label for the unidentified redactor or revising agency responsible for the Otho version; it does not assume that every difference was made by one historical person.

agents.name: G. L. Brook
notes: Co-editor of the 1963-1978 EETS source edition; not a medieval work-level creator.

agents.name: R. F. Leslie
notes: Co-editor of the 1963-1978 EETS source edition; not a medieval work-level creator.

scope.description: Authorship of the underlying Brut inherited by the Otho version

notes: This does not assign every Otho wording or abbreviation directly to Laȝamon.

scope.description: Underlying adaptation of Wace's Roman de Brut

notes: The relation concerns the underlying Brut; the Otho version adds a later layer of revision.
Current scholarship characterizes Otho as an abridgement and reworking, but intentional revision, scribal change, and later fire loss must not be conflated passage by passage.

scope.description: Editorial responsibility for the 1963-1978 immediate source edition, not medieval authorship
Editorial responsibility for the 1963-1978 immediate source edition, not medieval authorship

date_statements.display: Before or during production of the surviving Otho witness (1275–1299); exact revision date unknown
notes: The upper bound reflects the surviving witness, not proof that revision occurred in 1299. The underlying Brut was probably composed in the later twelfth century and before 1216, but that broader-work date is not silently assigned as the date of this version.

date_statements.display: Fourth quarter of the thirteenth century (1275–1299), in Cotton MS Otho C XIII
notes: This is the date of the surviving Otho manuscript, not the date of Laȝamon's underlying composition.
The British Library assigns the surviving manuscript an English origin. This does not establish a precise place for the Otho revision.
The narrative ranges beyond Britain but organizes a legendary history of the Britons from Trojan origins to the end of British rule.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: Latin bibliographic title forms and modern English editorial metadata do not make the medieval verse itself multilingual.

form_statements.description: A long Middle English verse history whose long lines combine alliteration and rhyme. The repository XML has 14,560 numbered L rows, of which 12,426 contain letters; 2,134 are marker-only rows recording additions, lacunae, or damage rather than recoverable verse text.

genre_statements.label: Historiography
notes: The poem reshapes legendary and historical traditions; the label does not imply modern critical historiography.

subject_statements.label: Legendary history of Britain and the Britons

scope.description: The Arthurian reign, wars, and fall within the larger Brut history

summaries.value: The Otho version retells the legendary history of Britain from Brutus and Trojan origins through successive rulers, invasions, Arthur's reign and fall, and the ending of British rule. It preserves Laȝamon's verse adaptation of Wace's Roman de Brut in a later, substantially abridged and reworked form whose diction is often modernized. The surviving manuscript is also severely damaged by the 1731 Cotton Library fire, so deliberate condensation, scribal revision, and later physical loss remain distinct explanatory layers.

This record describes the Otho version only. Cotton Otho C XIII is its surviving witness. Cotton Caligula A IX transmits the longer, more conservative version and remains a collateral comparison source, not a coequal witness to every Otho reading. Brook and Leslie's edition prints both streams; OTA and U-M separately encode the Otho stream while omitting edition front matter, back matter, notes, and illustrations.

extent.description: One counted verse work. The repository XML contains 14,560 unique consecutively numbered L rows, 12,426 letter-bearing rows, 2,134 marker-only rows, 146 DIV1 elements explicitly labeled supplied line groups, and 412 PB milestones for odd-numbered source-edition pages 3-839. The verse_lines value is therefore a transparent count of letter-bearing encoded rows, not a claim for the complete intrinsic length of the damaged Otho version.

content_parts.label: Laȝamon’s Brut, Otho version
content_parts.summary: The surviving Otho-version stream represented by the repository encoding, with extensive lacuna and damage notation.

locators.value: entire surviving manuscript; 144 folios in the current catalog, exact text-to-pencil-foliation crosswalk pending

notes: No lower-level authorial contents structure is asserted. The XML's 146 supplied divisions are intentionally not represented as intellectual parts.

The first leaf and several later leaves were already missing or damaged in Madden's nineteenth-century account; exact present folio boundaries must be established from a complete current surrogate or institutional response.

related_works.title: Laȝamon’s Brut
notes: The abstract work has two materially different surviving versions, Caligula and Otho. This record fixes the boundary at Otho.

related_works.title: Roman de Brut
notes: Wace's poem is the principal immediate literary source of Laȝamon's underlying Brut. This relation does not identify a particular physical exemplar or attribute the later Otho reworking to Wace.

evidence.summary: Primary local evidence for the U-M identifier and title, Laȝamon attribution, legacy date, source-edition statement, Wace relationship, electronic omissions, editorial-symbol note, complete encoded Otho stream, supplied divisions, and printed-page milestones.

evidence.citation: Target-scoped structural inspection of LayOtho.lineated.xml at pinned CME commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.summary: Counts 14,560 consecutively numbered L rows, including 12,426 letter-bearing and 2,134 marker-only rows; 146 explicitly supplied line-range divisions; and 412 source-edition page milestones numbered 3-839. This supports an encoding-specific extent while preventing damage markers or navigation groups from becoming intrinsic verse lines or authorial chapters.

evidence.citation: Character-normalized comparison of OTA 0085, OTA 1682 Otho, and U-M/CME LayOtho streams.
evidence.summary: After applying the documented legacy character and delimiter mappings, all 14,560 OTA 0085 Otho rows match OTA 1682. After stripping whitespace introduced around U-M page milestones, 14,559 of 14,560 U-M rows match OTA 1682; line 7574 is the only substantive difference, where U-M omits the Otho stream's thorn and semicolon after ‘endede’.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library Digital Collections, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse, LayOtho, Layamon's Brut (British Museum Ms. Cotton Otho C.XIII).
evidence.summary: Official item evidence for the title, author, Brook-Leslie publication statement, U-1682-C identity, public interface, supplied 100-line navigation, and item-level No Copyright-United States display.

evidence.citation: Nelson Goering, ‘Metrical Resolution in Early Middle English,’ in Prosody in Medieval English and Norse (British Academy / Oxford University Press, 2023), pp. 126-145, especially section 7.2.
evidence.summary: Current open scholarly synthesis supporting the modern-title boundary, two-version distinction, Otho's later abridgement and reworking, manuscript and underlying-work chronology, wording modernization, fire-loss distinction, and long-line alliterative/rhymed form.

evidence.citation: British Library, Cotton MS Otho C XIII, Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, record 040-001102891.
evidence.summary: Official evidence for the current shelfmark, 1275-1299 date, English origin, Middle English language, 144-folio parchment extent, 1731 fire damage, current access restriction, and Brook-Leslie and other Otho scholarship.

evidence.citation: CiNii Books, Laȝamon, Brut, edited from British Museum MS Cotton Caligula A. IX and Cotton Otho C. XIII, NCID BA02706965.
evidence.summary: Bibliographic evidence for G. L. Brook and R. F. Leslie, EETS Original Series 250 and 277, publication in 1963-1978, both named manuscript sources, and the two-volume division.

evidence.citation: LayOtho.lineated lineage evidence for OTA deposits 0085 and 1682 and their Otho payloads.
evidence.summary: Supports the legacy-to-current Otho stream continuity, two-file deposit boundary, exact transformation audit, encoding symbols, and omission of printed editorial matter.

open_questions.question: When and by whom was the Otho version abridged and reworked, and which differences reflect deliberate redaction rather than copying variation or later physical loss?
next_steps: Consult Christopher Cannon's work on style and authorship and Elizabeth Bryan's Otho study alongside newer scholarship.
Keep the anonymous-reviser label collective and descriptive until passage-level agency is demonstrated.
Do not infer revision date from the witness date more narrowly than the evidence allows.

open_questions.question: How do the 14,560 electronic rows and Brook-Leslie page milestones map to the current 144-folio Otho object, including lost and fire-damaged leaves?
next_steps: Request a complete color photographic surrogate of all available surfaces from the British Library and ask for the current pencil foliation.
Compare the delivered surrogate with Madden 1847 and Brook-Leslie before adding exact folio boundaries.
Do not invent a continuous 1r-144v work range from the catalog's folio count.

open_questions.question: What large-scale divisions, if any, can be attributed to the Otho version rather than to Brook-Leslie lineation or U-M navigation?
next_steps: Review Brook and Leslie's introductions and a complete Otho surrogate.
Do not convert the 146 supplied XML line groups into chapters.
Add stable intellectual parts only when manuscript or editorial evidence establishes their boundaries.

open_questions.question: What exact Otho-witness rubric, if any, supplies a historical title such as Libri Brutonum, and where does it survive in the damaged manuscript?
next_steps: Inspect a complete Otho surrogate or obtain an institutional folio reference.
Keep historical Latin title forms in explanatory notes rather than assigning an unverified Otho original title.

notes: The Caligula and Otho texts are versions of one abstract work but materially different textual streams; this record deliberately catalogs only Otho.

The Otho manuscript's copy date and region, the underlying Brut's probable composition period, the Brook-Leslie edition dates, the OTA dates, and the 1993 U-M date describe different layers and are not merged.
The 14,560 numbered electronic rows are practical locators. Marker-only rows and fire damage prevent that figure from serving as a simple intrinsic verse-line total.
Image availability, commissioned-image terms, modern-edition copyright, and digital-encoding rights are recorded by component in the lineage manifest; none is inferred from the medieval text's age.
